root: ^Above the chaos, above the stars, above the outermost sphere of the created world, there is light.
^Not the fitful glow of hellfire, not the cold glimmer of distant suns. This is light itself — the source, the origin, the uncreated radiance from which all lesser lights are borrowed.
^God sits enthroned in glory unapproachable. Around Him, rank upon rank of angels move in perfect harmony, their songs weaving through the crystalline spheres like golden threads through silk.
^God's gaze turns downward — through the spheres, through the firmament, through the vast gulf of Chaos — and fixes upon a single dark shape, wings beating against the void.
^The Almighty speaks, and His voice is the sound that underlies all other sounds.
^Behold. The Adversary approaches. He has passed through Chaos and even now draws near the borders of My new creation.
^The angels nearest the throne exchange glances. Among them, the Son — radiant beyond all others, seated at the right hand of the Father — listens in perfect stillness.
^He will reach Earth. He will find Man in the garden. And Man, deceived by the serpent's tongue, will fall.
^A murmur passes through the heavenly host. Fall? Man — the beloved creation, shaped in God's own image?

^Not by My constraint will Man stand or fall. I made him free — free to choose, free to err. Without that freedom, his obedience would be no obedience at all. A puppet cannot love. A slave cannot worship truly.
^(Listen carefully — there may be justice in these words.)
^(The same God who cast Satan down now speaks of freedom? Mark the contradiction.)
^(Observe the angels' reaction. Even Heaven has its doubts.)

c-0: ^There is weight in the words. Freedom without risk is no freedom at all. Even the angels can sense the terrible logic — that love compelled is not love, that virtue untested is merely innocence.
c-1: ^Freedom. The very word He denied Satan and the rebel angels. They chose — and were cast into fire for choosing. Now Man is given the same gift, the same perilous liberty.
^One wonders whether the outcome was ever truly in doubt.
c-2: ^The nearest seraphim hold their composure, but further from the throne, among the lesser choirs, there is unease. They remember the last time freedom was exercised in Heaven. They remember the war.
^Some of them were friends with the fallen. Some were brothers.

the_decree: ^God continues, and the heavens grow still.

^Man shall fall. This I foresee — not because I compel it, but because I see all ends from all beginnings. Satan's cunning will overwhelm Man's innocence. The fruit will be eaten. Death will enter the world.

^The word "death" ripples through the assembly. The angels have seen Death — the shapeless horror at Hell's gates. The thought of that creature loosed upon the gentle world below sends a shudder through even the highest powers.
^And what then, Father? Will Man be destroyed, as the rebels were cast down?
^The Son's voice is quiet but carries to every corner of Heaven. Every angel turns toward him.
^If Man falls, Justice demands a price. My law is broken, and law without consequence is no law at all. The penalty for disobedience is death.
^The pause holds all of Heaven in suspension.
^But I would not see Man destroyed utterly. If one could be found — one without sin, without stain — willing to take Man's punishment upon himself... then Justice and Mercy might both be satisfied.
^Silence falls over Heaven. The angels look at one another. God's gaze sweeps across the assembled host.
^Who among you will die for Man? Who will take upon themselves the weight of a world's sin, descend into death, and pay the price that Justice demands?
^(Watch the angels. See who speaks — and who stays silent.)
^(A rigged game. God already knows the answer. This is theatre.)
^(The weight of the question is real. To die for another — even an angel might fear that.)

c-0: ^The silence stretches. Michael, the warrior archangel, stands with bowed head. Gabriel, the messenger, looks toward the throne but does not speak. Raphael, Uriel — all are still.
^Not from cowardice. From awe. The magnitude of what is being asked surpasses even angelic comprehension.
^Then, from the right hand of the throne, a voice.
c-1: ^God, who sees all ends from all beginnings, who has already named the answer before the question is asked. Is this mercy, or merely the grandest performance in creation?
^But performance or not, the silence is real enough. Not one angel moves. Not one voice rises.
c-2: ^Every angel understands what is being asked. Not battle — they would rush to battle. Not sacrifice in the abstract. But death. True death. The cessation of immortal being. An angel dying for creatures made of dust.
^The silence is not hesitation. It is horror.
^And then, into that silence, a voice speaks.

the_volunteer: ^The words fall like light into darkness. Every angel turns. The Son rises from his seat at God's right hand, and His radiance fills Heaven until even the seraphim shield their eyes.

^I will take Man's nature upon myself. I will be born among them, live as they live, know hunger and weariness and sorrow. And when the time comes, I will die — truly die — to pay the debt that Justice demands.
^God regards His Son. And in that look — if such a thing can be said of the Almighty — there is something that might be grief. Or pride. Or love so vast it encompasses both.
^You know what you offer. The pain will be real. The death will be real. For a time, even I will turn My face from you.
^I know. But Man is worth the price. Your creation is worth preserving, even at this cost. And through death, I will defeat Death itself — the very monster Satan has unleashed.
^(This is love beyond comprehension. Whatever else Heaven may be, this sacrifice is real.)
^(Noble words. But this too was foreseen — planned before the world began. Can a scripted sacrifice truly be called sacrifice?)
^(Notice the symmetry: Satan volunteered to corrupt Man. The Son volunteers to save him. Two acts of will, mirrored and opposed.)

c-0: ^The angels erupt in praise. The music of the spheres swells to a crescendo that shakes the foundations of creation. Hosannas rise like golden fire, and for this moment, Heaven is what it was always meant to be — joy undiluted, love unrestrained.
^Even the memory of the fallen seems distant. Even the approaching shadow of Satan dims against this radiance.
c-1: ^The angels sing. They always sing. It is their nature, their purpose, their joy. But one might wonder — do any of them hear the echo? Satan too once volunteered. Satan too once stepped forward when others held back.
^The difference, perhaps, is in the direction: one stepped forward to destroy. The other steps forward to die.
^But both were willing. Both were brave. And both were known to God before they chose.
c-2: ^Two volunteers. Two acts of terrible courage. Satan, in the council of Hell, offering to brave Chaos alone to corrupt Man. The Son, in the council of Heaven, offering to descend to Earth and die for Man.
^Light and shadow. Mirror images. The same fierce will, turned to opposite ends.
^Heaven sings. And somewhere far below, Satan flies toward the very world the Son has just promised to save.

the_view_below: ^The celebration subsides. The songs fade to a gentle murmur. And God turns His gaze once more to the world below.
^Far beneath Heaven, past the sphere of stars and the crystalline orbits of the planets, Earth turns in its newborn beauty. Green and blue and gold, wreathed in clouds, cradled in light.
^And approaching it — a dark speck against the brightness of creation — Satan descends.
^He has cleared Chaos. He has found the outer shell of the universe. Even now he searches for a way in, circling the great sphere like a vulture over a walled garden.
^He will find the opening. He always does.

^The Son looks down at the world He has just promised to die for. The world that does not yet know it needs saving. The garden where two innocent beings walk in the cool of the evening, naming animals and tending flowers, unaware of the shadow falling toward them.
^Then let us watch. And when the time comes — when Man has fallen and the world lies broken — I will descend. Not in glory, but in flesh. Not to conquer, but to suffer.
^The light of Heaven blazes. Below, Satan draws ever closer to Paradise.
^The stage is set. The players move toward their appointed places. And the great drama of creation — fall and redemption, ruin and restoration — begins its inexorable unfolding.
